1 // 2 // Copyright 2024 Ettus Research, a National Instruments Brand 3 // 4 // SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later 5 // 6 7 #pragma once 8 9 #include 10 #include 11 12 namespace uhd { namespace rfnoc { 13 14 enum class fft_shift { NORMAL, REVERSE, NATURAL, BIT_REVERSE }; 15 enum class fft_direction { REVERSE, FORWARD }; 16 enum class fft_magnitude { COMPLEX, MAGNITUDE, MAGNITUDE_SQUARED }; 17 18 // Custom property keys 19 static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAGNITUDE = "magnitude"; 20 static const std::string PROP_KEY_DIRECTION = "direction"; 21 static const std::string PROP_KEY_LENGTH = "length"; 22 static const std::string PROP_KEY_FFT_SCALING = "fft_scaling"; 23 static const std::string PROP_KEY_FFT_SCALING_FACTOR = "fft_scaling_factor"; 24 static const std::string PROP_KEY_SHIFT_CONFIG = "shift_config"; 25 static const std::string PROP_KEY_BYPASS_MODE = "bypass_mode"; 26 static const std::string PROP_KEY_CP_INSERTION_LIST = "cp_insertion_list"; 27 static const std::string PROP_KEY_CP_REMOVAL_LIST = "cp_removal_list"; 28 static const std::string PROP_KEY_NIPC = "nipc"; 29 static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAX_LENGTH = "max_length"; 30 static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAX_CP_LENGTH = "max_cp_length"; 31 static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAX_CP_INSERTION_LIST_LENGTH = 32 "max_cp_insertion_list_length"; 33 static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAX_CP_REMOVAL_LIST_LENGTH = 34 "max_cp_removal_list_length"; 35 // clang-format off 36 /*! FFT Block (with Cyclic prefix insertion and removal) Control Class 37 * 38 * \ingroup rfnoc_blocks 39 * 40 * # Overview 41 * The FFT block is an RFNoC block that accepts signed complex 16-bit data 42 * at its input and computes the forward or reverse FFT of the input data, 43 * outputting signed complex 16-bit data at its output. 44 * 45 * The FFT length is configured via the length parameter, up to a maximum 46 * which depends on the instantiation on the FPGA. Use the function 47 * get_max_fft_length to determine the maximum supported FFT length. 48 * 49 * The length will be coerced to the closest power of two which is smaller 50 * than length. The block will output packets of the same length in the 51 * desired format as configured via the API. 52 * 53 * The block can be configured to add cyclic prefixes (typically when 54 * performing an inverse FFT, i.e. repeating a part of the generated time domain 55 * signal) or to remove cyclic prefixes (typically when performing a forward 56 * FFT, i.e. removing a part of the input time domain signal). This feature 57 * makes this block suitable for OFDM (de-)modulation. 58 * 59 * See RFNoC FFT block example 60 * in the uhd repository for how to use the API. 61 * 62 * 63 * 64 * # Features 65 66 * 67 * 1. Implements FFT and iFFT operations. 68 * - Based on Xilinx FFT IP core ( Xilinx Product Guide ) 69 * - Configured for continuous streaming through the FFT block 70 * - Runtime-configurable transform sizes: \f$N=2^m, m∈\{1,2,…16\}\f$ 71 * 2. Computes an (i)FFT across the full instantaneous bandwidth of any USRP 72 * through parallel multi-sample per FPGA clock cycle processing (added in UHD 73 * 4.9). 74 * 3. Cyclic prefix (CP) insertion and removal (added in UHD 4.8), enabling OFDM 75 * modulation 76 * - CP lengths can be varied as a function of the OFDM symbol index through 77 * a runtime-configurable "CP schedule" 78 * 4. Compile-time parameters enable / disable inclusion of FPGA logic to 79 * (examples) 80 * - compute the magnitude or square magnitude of the FFT output (useful for 81 * power spectral density estimation) 82 * - switch between computing or bypassing the (i)FFT at runtime 83 * - add/remove cyclic prefixes 84 * 5. The FFT window can span across multiple CHDR packets (added in UHD 4.8) 85 * 86 * 87 * # Theory of Operation 88 * 89 * The RFNoC FFT block can operate on signals at very wide bandwidth by processing 90 * multiple (NIPC , Number of Items Per Cycle) samples per cycle. That is, it can 91 * be used at master clock rates (MCR) that well exceed the FPGA clock rate. This 92 * capability is achieved by instantiating multiple, so called, (i)FFT pipelines 93 * in parallel. 94 * 95 * 96 * 97 * 98 * An (i)FFT pipeline computes either an iFFT or an FFT and can implement 99 * additional operations. Some of these operations require inclusion of FPGA logic 100 * at compile time. Some of these operations are run-time configurable as can be 101 * seen in the software interface documentation below. 102 * The RFNoC FFT block also offers the option to bypass 103 * the (i)FFT operations (pass-through of the input signal). 104 * 105 * An (i)FFT pipeline is internally structured as shown below. Some of the blocks 106 * are present only if the respective compile-time parameter is enabled. 107 * 108 * 109 * 110 * 111 * These are the operations each block implements: 112 * - CP removal: removal of a configurable number of samples preceding each FFT 113 * window, as required in an OFDM receiver 114 * - (i)FFT: FFT or iFFT operation, implemented by wrapping a Xilinx FFT block 115 * configured in pipelined streaming I/O architecture. 116 * - FFT (forward FFT): the frequency domain samples X(k) are computed from the 117 * time domain sequence x(n) through. 118 * \f$ X(k)=1/S ∑_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2πnk/N}, k=0,1,…,N-1 \f$
119 * S is the scaling factor. 120 * - iFFT (inverse FFT): 121 * \f$ x(n)=1/S ∑_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) e^{j2πnk/N}, n=0,1,…,N-1\f$ 122 * - Bit reversal: Xilinx IP is configured for outputs in bit-reversed order. This 123 * block arranges the output into natural order. 124 * - FFT shift: shifts DC to the center of the FFT output. 125 * - CP insertion: prepending a copy of a configurable number of last samples of an 126 * iFFT output as required in an OFDM transmitter. 127 * - (Squared) Magnitude (\f$| |, | |^2\f$): computes the (squared) magnitude of 128 * each output sample. 129 * 130 * 131 * # FPGA Compile-Time Configuration 132 * 133 * Users can configure the blocks' capabilities at FPGA compile-time by configuring a 134 * set of parameters in the RFNoC image core yaml file where the block is instantiated. 135 * 136 * ## User-Definable Parameters 137 * 138 * User-definable FPGA compile-time parameters are documented in the blocks' top-level 139 * system verilog file rfnoc_block_fft.sv and listed below. 140 * \snippet fpga/usrp3/lib/rfnoc/blocks/rfnoc_block_fft/rfnoc_block_fft.sv FFT_FPGA_DOCU 141 * 142 * The block description file fft.yml lists the default parameter values. 143 * \snippet host/include/uhd/rfnoc/blocks/fft.yml FFT_YML 144 * 145 * The table below summarizes the valid parameter ranges. A range without upper bound 146 * implies that the maximum value for a parameter is dictated by the available 147 * FPGA resources, but not by the FPGA IP design. 148 * 149 * | Compile-Time Parameter | Range | Comment | 150 * |------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------| 151 * | NIPC | \f$ 2^N, N=\{0, 1, 2, ...\}\f$ | | 152 * | NUM_PORTS | 1, 2, 3, ... | Must be integer-divisible by NUM_CORES | 153 * | NUM_CORES | 1, 2, 3, ... | | 154 * | MAX_FFT_SIZE_LOG2 | 10, 11,…,16 | 1k FFT … 64 k FFT | 155 * | EN_CP_REMOVAL | 0, 1 | | 156 * | EN_CP_INSERTION | 0, 1 | | 157 * | MAX_CP_LIST_LEN_INS_LOG2 | 1, 2, 3, ... | | 158 * | MAX_CP_LIST_LEN_REM_LOG2 | 1, 2, 3, ... | | 159 * | CP_INSERTION_REPEAT | 0, 1 | | 160 * | CP_REMOVAL_REPEAT | 0, 1 | | 161 * | EN_FFT_BYPASS | 0, 1 | | 162 * | EN_FFT_ORDER | 0, 1 | | 163 * | EN_MAGNITUDE | 0, 1 | | 164 * | EN_MAGNITUDE_SQ | 0, 1 | | 165 * | USE_APPROX_MAG | 0, 1 | Estimate of the magnitude that saves FPGA resources. | 166 * 167 * ## Choosing Parameter Values 168 * 169 * It is important to understand the high-level architecture of the RFNoC FFT 170 * block to pick proper compile-time parameters. The figure below introduces the 171 * different components that an RFNoC FFT block is composed of. 172 * 173 * 174 * 175 * \b NUM_CORES, \b NUM_PORTS, \b NIPC 176 * 177 * These parameters allow to reduce FPGA resource utilization by exploiting that 178 * multiple channels may be processed using the same compile-time or run-time 179 * parameters. 180 * 181 * An RFNoC FFT block can be configured to contain multiple (i)FFT pipelines. 182 * Multiple (i)FFT pipelines are useful to process multiple channels within a 183 * single RFNoC FFT block. This is more resource-efficient compared to processing 184 * each channel in a dedicated RFNoC FFT block. More specifically, an RFNoC FFT 185 * is internally sub-divided into one or multiple FFT cores. Each FFT core is 186 * further sub divided into one or multiple FFT pipeline wrappers. Finally, each 187 * FFT pipeline wrapper can contain one or multiple FFT pipelines as shown in the 188 * figure above. 189 * 190 * All **FFT cores** share the same compile time configuration but can have 191 * individual runtime configurations. For instance, the FFT direction and size or 192 * the CP length may be chosen differently per FFT core, at runtime. The maximum 193 * FFT size on the other hand is determined at compile time and shared across all 194 * FFT cores. The number of input ports NUM_PORTS to the RFnoC FFT block must be 195 * integer-divisible by the number of FFT cores. The first set of 196 * NUM_CHAN=NUM_PORTS / NUM_CORES ports will be processed by the first FFT core, 197 * the second set of NUM_PORTS / NUM_CORES ports by the second FFT core and so 198 * forth. 199 * 200 * All channels allocated to an FFT core must be used simultaneously, 201 * otherwise the FFT core will stall. 202 * 203 * Each channel processed by an FFT core is assigned its dedicated FFT pipeline 204 * wrapper. The parameter NIPC controls the number samples per FPGA clock cycle 205 * to be processed by the FFT pipeline wrapper. The number of FFT pipelines per 206 * FFT core equals NIPC. NIPC can be computed from ceil(MCR / CE). CE is the 207 * compute engine clock rate (see: 208 * https://kb.ettus.com/RFNoC_Frequently_Asked_Questions#What_are_the_clock_frequencies.3F) 209 * and MCR the master clock rate. Also, some margin is needed. That is, as a 210 * rule of thumb ceil(MCR / CE) / (MCR / CE) > 1.05 should be satisfied. 211 * 212 * Example: Assume we are using an MCR of 491.52 MS/s on USRP X410 ( CE = 213 * 266.667 MHz). MCR / CE = 1.84, ceil(MCR / CE)=2 and ceil(MCR / CE) / 214 * (MCR / CE) = 1.09. NIPC=2 is sufficient in that case. 215 * 216 * Choosing compile-time configurations to minimize FPGA resource utilization 217 * . 218 * 219 * - Enable only the logic that is required (example: CP removal / addition, 220 * (squared) magnitude computation, pick the minimal maximum FFT size required 221 * by the application) 222 * - Process as many channels as possible within a single RFNoC FFT block and FFT 223 * core. Suppose you want to process N channels. Then the most 224 * resource-efficient configuration is to use a single RFNoC FFT block with 225 * NUM_PORTS = N and NUM_CORES = 1. Of course, this is only possible if all 226 * channels share the same compile and runtime configuration and are processed 227 * simultaneously. 228 * 229 * Dependent compile-time parameters 230 * - EN_FFT_ORDER and EN_CP_INSERTION: The bit reversal, FFT shift and CP 231 * insertion blocks share FPGA logic. 232 * - EN_CP_INSERTION = 1 requires EN_FFT_ORDER = 1, otherwise the FPGA compile will 233 * fail. 234 * 235 * 236 * ## Example FPGA Compile-Time Configuration 237 * 238 * An example of a typical OFDM transmitter and receiver configuration covering 239 * 400 MHz of RF bandwidth on an USRP X410 is shown below. A maximum 8192 size 240 * FFT is configured leading to a minimal subcarrier spacing of 60 kHz. At the 241 * transmit side, cyclic prefix insertion is enabled. At the receive side, cyclic 242 * prefix removal is enabled. 243 * 244 * | Compile-Time Parameter | Value Transmitter | Value Receiver | 245 * |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| 246 * | NIPC | 2 | 2 | 247 * | NUM_PORTS | 2 | 2 | 248 * | MAX_FFT_SIZE_LOG2 | 13 | 13 | 249 * | EN_CP_REMOVAL | 0 | 1 | 250 * | EN_CP_INSERTION | 1 | 0 | 251 * | EN_MAGNITUDE | 0 | 0 | 252 * | EN_MAGNITUDE_SQ | 0 | 0 | 253 * 254 * # Advanced Topics 255 * 256 * ## Xilinx FFT Block Configuration 257 * 258 * The RFNoC FFT block is based on a Xilinx FFT block. Different configurations 259 * for different maximum FFT sizes have been prepared. The respective Xilinx 260 * settings for IP core generation can be obtained by opening the respective .xci 261 * files in Xilinx Vivado (example for the maximal 16k FFT: 263 * xfft_16k_16b.xci ) 264 * 265 * An example is shown below. 266 * 267 * 268 * 269 * ## Implementing Different Subcarrier Spacings in OFDM Systems 270 * 271 * When implementing an OFDM system, there is a target subcarrier spacing 272 * (inter FFT frequency bin spacing), typically. 273 * 274 * Case 1 : FPGA design does not implement sample rate conversion 275 * through DUC/DDC 276 * 277 * 278 * 279 * Assume the (i)FFT block is directly connected to the radio block, i.e., the 280 * input rate to the FFT block or the output rate of the FFT block equals the 281 * master clock rate (MCR). The subcarrier spacing \f$ Δf_{sc} \f$ is
282 * 283 *
284 * \f$ Δf_{sc} = MCR / N_{FFT} \f$ 285 *
286 * 287 * For instance, on USRP X410 a master clock rate of 245.76 MHz and an FFT size 288 * \f$ N_{FFT} \f$ of 8192 result in a \f$ Δf_{sc}=30 kHz \f$ subcarrier 289 * spacing. 290 * 291 * Case 2 : FPGA design does implement sample rate conversion 292 * through DUC/DDC 293 * 294 * 295 * 296 * Assume the (i)FFT block is connected to the radio block through a DDC/DUC 297 * block implementing a sample rate conversion factor R. The subcarrier spacing 298 * \f$ Δf_{sc} \f$ is 299 * 300 *
301 * \f$ Δf_{sc} = MCR / {R N_{FFT}} \f$ 302 *
303 * 304 * For instance, on USRP X410 a master clock rate of 245.76 MHz, an FFT size of 305 * 4096 and a sample rate conversion factor of R=2 result in a 30 kHz subcarrier 306 * spacing. 307 * 308 * ## Approximate Processing Latency 309 * 310 * The approximate processing latency of the RFNoC FFT block in cycles 311 * \f$ N_{Lat} \f$ is
312 * 313 *
314 * \f$ N_{Lat}≈NIPC⋅(N_{FFT}+N_{CP}) \f$ 315 *
316 * 317 * The latency \f$ T_{Lat} \f$ in seconds depends on the compute engine clock rate 318 * CE
319 * 320 *
321 * \f$ T_{Lat}≈N_{Lat}/CE \f$ 322 *
323 * 324 * Example for a 400 MHz USRP X410 OFDM configuration: 325 * - MCR = 491.52 MS/s 326 * - CE = 266.667 MHz 327 * - NIPC = 2 328 * - N_FFT=8192, N_CP=576 (subcarrier spacing = 60 kHz)
329 * 330 *
331 * \f$ T_{Lat}≈N_{Lat}/CE≈17536/(266.667⋅10e6) s≈66us \f$ 332 *
333 * 334 * ## Time Stamping 335 * 336 * CHDR data packets containing FFT input or output data can comprise time 337 * stamps. The figure below illustrates how these are computed for the example: 338 * - OFDM configuration – cyclic prefixes are added or removed 339 * - Burst containing 2 OFDM symbols 340 * 341 * 342 * 343 * 1. For the first packet in a burst:
344 * The incoming packet contains a time stamp for the start of the burst 345 * that is provided by either the host or the radio block. This time stamp 346 * is in units of the master clock rate. The packet at the output of the 347 * (i)FFT block contains this exact same timestamp without modifications. 348 * In other words, the FFT block doesn't adjust the timestamp to 349 * compensate for the insertion or removal of the cyclic prefix. 350 * 2. For subsequent packets in a burst:
351 * The FFT block increments the time stamp for the start of the burst by 352 * the number of samples per packet (SPP). 353 * 354 * 355 * ## Behavior In Case of Streaming Errors 356 * 357 * It is possible that transmit or receive packets are dropped on the transport 358 * link that connects USRP and host computer. This section discusses how to 359 * detect and handle 360 * these situations. 361 * 362 * RX Streaming (FFT operation) 363 * 1. After each receive call, check for the out_of_sequence flag in the stream 364 * metadata. 365 * 2. If the receive call metadata indicates the data is out of sequence, then 366 * the application can use the time_spec to determine where this data goes in 367 * the sequence of expected data and take appropriate action (e.g., discard 368 * the data up to the start of the next symbol). 369 * 3. Streaming can continue as before after this point. 370 * 371 * TX Streaming (iFFT operation) 372 * 1. The application sets up a thread to receive and monitor asynchronous 373 * messages ( rev_async_msg() ) from the TX streamer. 374 * 2. When an async message reports an underflow or sequence error, then the 375 * application should end the current burst and wait for the burst ack. 376 * - The FFT block will automatically end the data stream and flush any data 377 * out of the FFT block. This may cause the last packet to be sent with 378 * corrupted data, since part of an input symbol was lost, but should 379 * leave the FFT block in a good state for the next burst. 380 * - If the application doesn't receive a burst ack, then it should reset 381 * the FFT block, and reconfigure it before proceeding. 382 * 3. After this the application can restart streaming from where it left off 383 * or at some later point in time as needed by the application. 384 * 385 * 386 * 387 * ## Timed Commands 388 * The FFT block does not support timed commands. That is, none of its 389 * runtime-configurable properties can be configured in a deterministically timed 390 * fashion. 391 * 392 * ## Configuring the CHDR Packet Lengths for Initial Debugging 393 * Debugging can be simplified by configuring the CHDR packet length such that 394 * an integer multiple of the CHDR packet length equals the FFT size. 395 * The FFT functionality does not break if this recommendation is not followed. 396 * Example: In case of a 4096 point FFT, the CHDR packet length could be chosen 397 * equal to 1024 samples per packet. 398 * 399 * 400 * # Known Limitations 401 * 1. MAX_FFT_SIZE_LOG2 = 16 (64k FFT) can be configured but has not been 402 * validated in hardware 403 * 2. MAX_FFT_SIZE_LOG2 = 15 (32k FFT): using this maximum-size FFT 404 * implementation in an 8 point FFT configuration does not work properly. 405 * 406 */ 407 // clang-format on 408 class UHD_API fft_block_control : public uhd::rfnoc::noc_block_base 409 { 410 public: 411 RFNOC_DECLARE_BLOCK(fft_block_control) 412 413 //! Register addresses 414 static const uint32_t REG_COMPAT_ADDR; 415 static const uint32_t REG_PORT_CONFIG_ADDR; 416 static const uint32_t REG_CAPABILITIES_ADDR; 417 static const uint32_t REG_CAPABILITIES2_ADDR; 418 static const uint32_t REG_RESET_ADDR; 419 static const uint32_t REG_LENGTH_LOG2_ADDR; 420 static const uint32_t REG_SCALING_ADDR; 421 static const uint32_t REG_DIRECTION_ADDR; 422 static const uint32_t REG_CP_INS_LEN_ADDR; 423 static const uint32_t REG_CP_INS_LIST_LOAD_ADDR; 424 static const uint32_t REG_CP_INS_LIST_CLR_ADDR; 425 static const uint32_t REG_CP_INS_LIST_OCC_ADDR; 426 static const uint32_t REG_CP_REM_LEN_ADDR; 427 static const uint32_t REG_CP_REM_LIST_LOAD_ADDR; 428 static const uint32_t REG_CP_REM_LIST_CLR_ADDR; 429 static const uint32_t REG_CP_REM_LIST_OCC_ADDR; 430 static const uint32_t REG_OVERFLOW_ADDR; 431 static const uint32_t REG_BYPASS_ADDR; 432 static const uint32_t REG_ORDER_ADDR; 433 static const uint32_t REG_MAGNITUDE_ADDR; 434 435 //! Register addresses of the FFT block version 1 436 static const uint32_t REG_RESET_ADDR_V1; 437 static const uint32_t REG_LENGTH_LOG2_ADDR_V1; 438 static const uint32_t REG_MAGNITUDE_ADDR_V1; 439 static const uint32_t REG_DIRECTION_ADDR_V1; 440 static const uint32_t REG_SCALING_ADDR_V1; 441 static const uint32_t REG_ORDER_ADDR_V1; 442 443 /*! Set the FFT direction 444 * 445 * Sets the direction of the FFT, either forward (FORWARD) or inverse 446 * (REVERSE). 447 * 448 * \param direction FFT direction 449 */ 450 virtual void set_direction(const fft_direction direction) = 0; 451 452 /*! Get the FFT direction 453 * 454 * Returns the current direction of the FFT. 455 * 456 * \returns FFT direction 457 */ 458 virtual fft_direction get_direction() const = 0; 459 460 /*! Set the format of the returned FFT output data 461 * 462 * Sets the format in which the FFT output data is returned. The following 463 * formats are supported: 464 * 465 * * amplitude/phase data (COMPLEX) 466 * * magnitude data (MAGNITUDE) 467 * * mag-squared data (MAGNITUDE_SQUARED) 468 * 469 * \param magnitude Format of the returned FFT output data 470 */ 471 virtual void set_magnitude(const fft_magnitude magnitude) = 0; 472 473 /*! Get the format of the returned FFT output data 474 * 475 * Returns the current output format of the FFT data. 476 * 477 * \returns Format of the returned FFT output data 478 */ 479 virtual fft_magnitude get_magnitude() const = 0; 480 481 /*! Set the shift configuration of the output FFT data 482 * 483 * Sets how the FFT output data is shifted (to get the zero frequency bin 484 * to the center of the output data). The following output data shift 485 * configurations are supported: 486 * 487 * * Negative frequencies first, then positive frequencies (NORMAL) 488 * * Positive frequencies first, then negative frequencies (REVERSE) 489 * * Bypass the shift altogether, leaving the zero frequency bin 490 * returned first (NATURAL). 491 * * Bit-reversed order (BIT_REVERSE). This is typically the native 492 * order output by the FFT. In other words, selecting this mode may 493 * mean that the data from the FFT is not reordered. In this mode, 494 * the indices of the FFT are bit-reversed. For example, for a size 495 * 16 FFT, instead of outputting bins in the order 0000, 0001, 0010, 496 * 0011, etc., it outputs bins 0000, 1000, 0100, 1100, etc. 497 * 498 * \param shift Configuration for shifting FFT output data 499 */ 500 virtual void set_shift_config(const fft_shift shift) = 0; 501 502 /*! Get the shift configuration of the output FFT data 503 * 504 * Returns the current shift configuration of the output FFT data. 505 * 506 * \returns Shift configuration of the output FFT data 507 */ 508 virtual fft_shift get_shift_config() const = 0; 509 510 /*! Set the scaling factor for the FFT block 511 * 512 * This is a convenience function which can be used instead of 513 * set_scaling(). Based on the given factor, it automatically sets the 514 * scaling mask by evenly distributing the scaling across the active FFT 515 * stages 516 * 517 * Examples: 518 * - factor = 1.0 -> no scaling 519 * - factor = 0.5 (1/2) -> the scaling will be set to 0b000000000001 520 * -> scale by 2 in the first FFT stage 521 * - factor = 0.0625 (1/16) -> the scaling will be set to 0b000000001010 522 * -> scale by 4 in both the first and the second FFT stage 523 * - factor = 0.03125 (1/32) -> the scaling will be set to 0b000000011010 524 * -> scale by 4 in both the first and the second FFT stage 525 * and by 2 in the third FFT stage 526 * 527 * \param factor Desired scaling factor 528 */ 529 virtual void set_scaling_factor(const double factor) = 0; 530 531 /*! Set the scaling schedule for the FFT block 532 * 533 * Sets the scaling for each stage of the FFT. This value maps directly 534 * to the scale schedule field in the configuration channel data that is 535 * passed to the Xilinx AXI FFT IP. For more information on the format 536 * of this data, see Xilinx document PG109, Fast Fourier Transform 537 * LogiCORE IP Product Guide. 538 * 539 * Examples: 540 * - scaling = 0b000000000000 -> no scaling 541 * - scaling = 0b000000000001 -> scale by 2 in the first FFT stage 542 * - scaling = 0b000000001010 -> scale by 4 in both the first and the second 543 * FFT stage 544 * - scaling = 0b000000011010 -> scale by 4 in both the first and the second 545 * FFT stage and by 2 in the third FFT stage 546 * 547 * \param scaling Scaling schedule for the FFT block 548 */ 549 virtual void set_scaling(const uint32_t scaling) = 0; 550 551 /*! Get the scaling schedule for the FFT block 552 * 553 * Returns the current scaling schedule for the FFT block. 554 * 555 * \returns Scaling schedule for the FFT block 556 */ 557 virtual uint32_t get_scaling() const = 0; 558 559 /*! Set the length of the FFT 560 * 561 * Sets the length of the FFT in number of samples. Note that the FFT 562 * IP requires a power-of-two number of samples; the incoming value will 563 * be coerced to the closest smaller power of two. 564 * 565 * \param length Desired FFT length 566 */ 567 virtual void set_length(const uint32_t length) = 0; 568 569 /*! Get the length of the FFT 570 * 571 * Returns the current length of the FFT. 572 * 573 * \returns Current FFT length 574 */ 575 virtual uint32_t get_length() const = 0; 576 577 /*! Set the bypass mode of the FFT 578 * 579 * Enable FFT bypass mode. Set true to enable, false to disable. When 580 * enabled, the data is passed through without any FFT processing. Note that 581 * cyclic prefix insertion will not work in bypass mode, because insertion 582 * is handled by the FFT core, but cyclic prefix removal will work. 583 * 584 * \param bypass FFT bypass moe 585 */ 586 virtual void set_bypass_mode(const bool bypass) = 0; 587 588 /*! Get the bypass mode of the FFT 589 * 590 * Returns the current bypass mode. 591 * 592 * \returns Current FFT bypass mode 593 */ 594 virtual bool get_bypass_mode() const = 0; 595 596 /*! Get the number of items per clock cycle (NIPC) 597 * 598 * Returns the number of items per clock cycle (NIPC) that this block is 599 * configured to process. Packet sizes and cyclic prefix lengths must a 600 * multiple of this value. 601 * 602 * \returns NIPC 603 */ 604 virtual uint32_t get_nipc() const = 0; 605 606 /*! Get the maximum supported length of the FFT 607 * 608 * Returns the maximum supported length of the FFT. 609 * 610 * \returns Maximum supported FFT length 611 */ 612 virtual uint32_t get_max_length() const = 0; 613 614 /*! Get the maximum supported cyclic prefix length 615 * 616 * Returns the maximum supported cyclic prefix length. 617 * 618 * \returns Maximum supported cyclic prefix length 619 */ 620 virtual uint32_t get_max_cp_length() const = 0; 621 622 /*! Get the maximum supported number of values that can be written to the 623 * cyclic prefix removal list 624 * 625 * Returns the maximum supported number of values that can be written to 626 * the cyclic prefix removal list. 627 * 628 * \returns Maximum number of values for the cyclic prefix removal list 629 */ 630 virtual uint32_t get_max_cp_removal_list_length() const = 0; 631 632 /*! Get the maximum supported number of values that can be written to the 633 * cyclic prefix insertion list 634 * 635 * Returns the maximum supported number of values that can be written to 636 * the cyclic prefix insertion list. 637 * 638 * \returns Maximum number of values for the cyclic prefix insertion list 639 */ 640 virtual uint32_t get_max_cp_insertion_list_length() const = 0; 641 642 /*! Load values to the cyclic prefix insertion list. 643 * 644 * Loads values to the cyclic prefix insertion list. Each value represents 645 * the length of a cyclic prefix that is prepended to the output of the 646 * (typically inverse) FFT operation (typically the time domain signal). If 647 * the length of the cyclic prefix insertion list is m, then the cyclic 648 * prefix length that is added to the output signal of symbol n is: 649 * 650 * cp_length[n] = cp_length[n mod m] 651 * 652 * \param cp_lengths The cyclic prefix lengths to be written to the list 653 */ 654 virtual void set_cp_insertion_list(const std::vector cp_lengths) = 0; 655 656 /*! Gets the values from the cyclic prefix insertion list. 657 * 658 * Gets values to the cyclic prefix insertion list. After initialization, 659 * no CP insertion values are configured. Use the function 660 * set_cp_insertion_list to set the values. 661 */ 662 virtual std::vector get_cp_insertion_list() const = 0; 663 664 /*! Load values to the cyclic prefix removal list. 665 * 666 * Loads values to the cyclic prefix removal list. Each value represents 667 * the length of a cyclic prefix that is removed from the input signal 668 * (typically the time domain signal) before performing the (typically 669 * forward) FFT operation. If the length of the cyclic prefix removal list 670 * is m, then the cyclic prefix length that is removed from the input signal 671 * of symbol n is: 672 * 673 * cp_length[n] = cp_length[n mod m] 674 * 675 * \param cp_lengths The cyclic prefix lengths to be written to the list 676 */ 677 virtual void set_cp_removal_list(const std::vector cp_lengths) = 0; 678 679 /*! Gets the values from the cyclic prefix removal list. 680 * 681 * Gets values to the cyclic prefix removal list. After initialization, 682 * no CP removal values are configured. Use the function 683 * set_cp_removal_list to set the values. 684 */ 685 virtual std::vector get_cp_removal_list() const = 0; 686 }; 687 688 }} // namespace uhd::rfnoc 689

// // Copyright 2024 Ettus Research, a National Instruments Brand // // SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-3.0-or-later // #pragma once #include #include namespace uhd { namespace rfnoc { enum class fft_shift { NORMAL, REVERSE, NATURAL, BIT_REVERSE }; enum class fft_direction { REVERSE, FORWARD }; enum class fft_magnitude { COMPLEX, MAGNITUDE, MAGNITUDE_SQUARED }; // Custom property keys static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAGNITUDE = "magnitude"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_DIRECTION = "direction"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_LENGTH = "length"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_FFT_SCALING = "fft_scaling"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_FFT_SCALING_FACTOR = "fft_scaling_factor"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_SHIFT_CONFIG = "shift_config"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_BYPASS_MODE = "bypass_mode"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_CP_INSERTION_LIST = "cp_insertion_list"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_CP_REMOVAL_LIST = "cp_removal_list"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_NIPC = "nipc"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAX_LENGTH = "max_length"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAX_CP_LENGTH = "max_cp_length"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAX_CP_INSERTION_LIST_LENGTH = "max_cp_insertion_list_length"; static const std::string PROP_KEY_MAX_CP_REMOVAL_LIST_LENGTH = "max_cp_removal_list_length"; // clang-format off /*! FFT Block (with Cyclic prefix insertion and removal) Control Class * * \ingroup rfnoc_blocks * * # Overview * The FFT block is an RFNoC block that accepts signed complex 16-bit data * at its input and computes the forward or reverse FFT of the input data, * outputting signed complex 16-bit data at its output. * * The FFT length is configured via the length parameter, up to a maximum * which depends on the instantiation on the FPGA. Use the function * get_max_fft_length to determine the maximum supported FFT length. * * The length will be coerced to the closest power of two which is smaller * than length. The block will output packets of the same length in the * desired format as configured via the API. * * The block can be configured to add cyclic prefixes (typically when * performing an inverse FFT, i.e. repeating a part of the generated time domain * signal) or to remove cyclic prefixes (typically when performing a forward * FFT, i.e. removing a part of the input time domain signal). This feature * makes this block suitable for OFDM (de-)modulation. * * See RFNoC FFT block example * in the uhd repository for how to use the API. * * * * # Features * * 1. Implements FFT and iFFT operations. * - Based on Xilinx FFT IP core ( Xilinx Product Guide ) * - Configured for continuous streaming through the FFT block * - Runtime-configurable transform sizes: \f$N=2^m, m∈\{1,2,…16\}\f$ * 2. Computes an (i)FFT across the full instantaneous bandwidth of any USRP * through parallel multi-sample per FPGA clock cycle processing (added in UHD * 4.9). * 3. Cyclic prefix (CP) insertion and removal (added in UHD 4.8), enabling OFDM * modulation * - CP lengths can be varied as a function of the OFDM symbol index through * a runtime-configurable "CP schedule" * 4. Compile-time parameters enable / disable inclusion of FPGA logic to * (examples) * - compute the magnitude or square magnitude of the FFT output (useful for * power spectral density estimation) * - switch between computing or bypassing the (i)FFT at runtime * - add/remove cyclic prefixes * 5. The FFT window can span across multiple CHDR packets (added in UHD 4.8) * * * # Theory of Operation * * The RFNoC FFT block can operate on signals at very wide bandwidth by processing * multiple (NIPC , Number of Items Per Cycle) samples per cycle. That is, it can * be used at master clock rates (MCR) that well exceed the FPGA clock rate. This * capability is achieved by instantiating multiple, so called, (i)FFT pipelines * in parallel. * * * * * An (i)FFT pipeline computes either an iFFT or an FFT and can implement * additional operations. Some of these operations require inclusion of FPGA logic * at compile time. Some of these operations are run-time configurable as can be * seen in the software interface documentation below. * The RFNoC FFT block also offers the option to bypass * the (i)FFT operations (pass-through of the input signal). * * An (i)FFT pipeline is internally structured as shown below. Some of the blocks * are present only if the respective compile-time parameter is enabled. * * * * * These are the operations each block implements: * - CP removal: removal of a configurable number of samples preceding each FFT * window, as required in an OFDM receiver * - (i)FFT: FFT or iFFT operation, implemented by wrapping a Xilinx FFT block * configured in pipelined streaming I/O architecture. * - FFT (forward FFT): the frequency domain samples X(k) are computed from the * time domain sequence x(n) through. * \f$ X(k)=1/S ∑_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2πnk/N}, k=0,1,…,N-1 \f$
* S is the scaling factor. * - iFFT (inverse FFT): * \f$ x(n)=1/S ∑_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) e^{j2πnk/N}, n=0,1,…,N-1\f$ * - Bit reversal: Xilinx IP is configured for outputs in bit-reversed order. This * block arranges the output into natural order. * - FFT shift: shifts DC to the center of the FFT output. * - CP insertion: prepending a copy of a configurable number of last samples of an * iFFT output as required in an OFDM transmitter. * - (Squared) Magnitude (\f$| |, | |^2\f$): computes the (squared) magnitude of * each output sample. * * * # FPGA Compile-Time Configuration * * Users can configure the blocks' capabilities at FPGA compile-time by configuring a * set of parameters in the RFNoC image core yaml file where the block is instantiated. * * ## User-Definable Parameters * * User-definable FPGA compile-time parameters are documented in the blocks' top-level * system verilog file rfnoc_block_fft.sv and listed below. * \snippet fpga/usrp3/lib/rfnoc/blocks/rfnoc_block_fft/rfnoc_block_fft.sv FFT_FPGA_DOCU * * The block description file fft.yml lists the default parameter values. * \snippet host/include/uhd/rfnoc/blocks/fft.yml FFT_YML * * The table below summarizes the valid parameter ranges. A range without upper bound * implies that the maximum value for a parameter is dictated by the available * FPGA resources, but not by the FPGA IP design. * * | Compile-Time Parameter | Range | Comment | * |------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------| * | NIPC | \f$ 2^N, N=\{0, 1, 2, ...\}\f$ | | * | NUM_PORTS | 1, 2, 3, ... | Must be integer-divisible by NUM_CORES | * | NUM_CORES | 1, 2, 3, ... | | * | MAX_FFT_SIZE_LOG2 | 10, 11,…,16 | 1k FFT … 64 k FFT | * | EN_CP_REMOVAL | 0, 1 | | * | EN_CP_INSERTION | 0, 1 | | * | MAX_CP_LIST_LEN_INS_LOG2 | 1, 2, 3, ... | | * | MAX_CP_LIST_LEN_REM_LOG2 | 1, 2, 3, ... | | * | CP_INSERTION_REPEAT | 0, 1 | | * | CP_REMOVAL_REPEAT | 0, 1 | | * | EN_FFT_BYPASS | 0, 1 | | * | EN_FFT_ORDER | 0, 1 | | * | EN_MAGNITUDE | 0, 1 | | * | EN_MAGNITUDE_SQ | 0, 1 | | * | USE_APPROX_MAG | 0, 1 | Estimate of the magnitude that saves FPGA resources. | * * ## Choosing Parameter Values * * It is important to understand the high-level architecture of the RFNoC FFT * block to pick proper compile-time parameters. The figure below introduces the * different components that an RFNoC FFT block is composed of. * * * * \b NUM_CORES, \b NUM_PORTS, \b NIPC * * These parameters allow to reduce FPGA resource utilization by exploiting that * multiple channels may be processed using the same compile-time or run-time * parameters. * * An RFNoC FFT block can be configured to contain multiple (i)FFT pipelines. * Multiple (i)FFT pipelines are useful to process multiple channels within a * single RFNoC FFT block. This is more resource-efficient compared to processing * each channel in a dedicated RFNoC FFT block. More specifically, an RFNoC FFT * is internally sub-divided into one or multiple FFT cores. Each FFT core is * further sub divided into one or multiple FFT pipeline wrappers. Finally, each * FFT pipeline wrapper can contain one or multiple FFT pipelines as shown in the * figure above. * * All **FFT cores** share the same compile time configuration but can have * individual runtime configurations. For instance, the FFT direction and size or * the CP length may be chosen differently per FFT core, at runtime. The maximum * FFT size on the other hand is determined at compile time and shared across all * FFT cores. The number of input ports NUM_PORTS to the RFnoC FFT block must be * integer-divisible by the number of FFT cores. The first set of * NUM_CHAN=NUM_PORTS / NUM_CORES ports will be processed by the first FFT core, * the second set of NUM_PORTS / NUM_CORES ports by the second FFT core and so * forth. * * All channels allocated to an FFT core must be used simultaneously, * otherwise the FFT core will stall. * * Each channel processed by an FFT core is assigned its dedicated FFT pipeline * wrapper. The parameter NIPC controls the number samples per FPGA clock cycle * to be processed by the FFT pipeline wrapper. The number of FFT pipelines per * FFT core equals NIPC. NIPC can be computed from ceil(MCR / CE). CE is the * compute engine clock rate (see: * https://kb.ettus.com/RFNoC_Frequently_Asked_Questions#What_are_the_clock_frequencies.3F) * and MCR the master clock rate. Also, some margin is needed. That is, as a * rule of thumb ceil(MCR / CE) / (MCR / CE) > 1.05 should be satisfied. * * Example: Assume we are using an MCR of 491.52 MS/s on USRP X410 ( CE = * 266.667 MHz). MCR / CE = 1.84, ceil(MCR / CE)=2 and ceil(MCR / CE) / * (MCR / CE) = 1.09. NIPC=2 is sufficient in that case. * * Choosing compile-time configurations to minimize FPGA resource utilization * . * * - Enable only the logic that is required (example: CP removal / addition, * (squared) magnitude computation, pick the minimal maximum FFT size required * by the application) * - Process as many channels as possible within a single RFNoC FFT block and FFT * core. Suppose you want to process N channels. Then the most * resource-efficient configuration is to use a single RFNoC FFT block with * NUM_PORTS = N and NUM_CORES = 1. Of course, this is only possible if all * channels share the same compile and runtime configuration and are processed * simultaneously. * * Dependent compile-time parameters * - EN_FFT_ORDER and EN_CP_INSERTION: The bit reversal, FFT shift and CP * insertion blocks share FPGA logic. * - EN_CP_INSERTION = 1 requires EN_FFT_ORDER = 1, otherwise the FPGA compile will * fail. * * * ## Example FPGA Compile-Time Configuration * * An example of a typical OFDM transmitter and receiver configuration covering * 400 MHz of RF bandwidth on an USRP X410 is shown below. A maximum 8192 size * FFT is configured leading to a minimal subcarrier spacing of 60 kHz. At the * transmit side, cyclic prefix insertion is enabled. At the receive side, cyclic * prefix removal is enabled. * * | Compile-Time Parameter | Value Transmitter | Value Receiver | * |------------------------|-------------------|----------------| * | NIPC | 2 | 2 | * | NUM_PORTS | 2 | 2 | * | MAX_FFT_SIZE_LOG2 | 13 | 13 | * | EN_CP_REMOVAL | 0 | 1 | * | EN_CP_INSERTION | 1 | 0 | * | EN_MAGNITUDE | 0 | 0 | * | EN_MAGNITUDE_SQ | 0 | 0 | * * # Advanced Topics * * ## Xilinx FFT Block Configuration * * The RFNoC FFT block is based on a Xilinx FFT block. Different configurations * for different maximum FFT sizes have been prepared. The respective Xilinx * settings for IP core generation can be obtained by opening the respective .xci * files in Xilinx Vivado (example for the maximal 16k FFT: * xfft_16k_16b.xci ) * * An example is shown below. * * * * ## Implementing Different Subcarrier Spacings in OFDM Systems * * When implementing an OFDM system, there is a target subcarrier spacing * (inter FFT frequency bin spacing), typically. * * Case 1 : FPGA design does not implement sample rate conversion * through DUC/DDC * * * * Assume the (i)FFT block is directly connected to the radio block, i.e., the * input rate to the FFT block or the output rate of the FFT block equals the * master clock rate (MCR). The subcarrier spacing \f$ Δf_{sc} \f$ is
* *
* \f$ Δf_{sc} = MCR / N_{FFT} \f$ *
* * For instance, on USRP X410 a master clock rate of 245.76 MHz and an FFT size * \f$ N_{FFT} \f$ of 8192 result in a \f$ Δf_{sc}=30 kHz \f$ subcarrier * spacing. * * Case 2 : FPGA design does implement sample rate conversion * through DUC/DDC * * * * Assume the (i)FFT block is connected to the radio block through a DDC/DUC * block implementing a sample rate conversion factor R. The subcarrier spacing * \f$ Δf_{sc} \f$ is * *
* \f$ Δf_{sc} = MCR / {R N_{FFT}} \f$ *
* * For instance, on USRP X410 a master clock rate of 245.76 MHz, an FFT size of * 4096 and a sample rate conversion factor of R=2 result in a 30 kHz subcarrier * spacing. * * ## Approximate Processing Latency * * The approximate processing latency of the RFNoC FFT block in cycles * \f$ N_{Lat} \f$ is
* *
* \f$ N_{Lat}≈NIPC⋅(N_{FFT}+N_{CP}) \f$ *
* * The latency \f$ T_{Lat} \f$ in seconds depends on the compute engine clock rate * CE
* *
* \f$ T_{Lat}≈N_{Lat}/CE \f$ *
* * Example for a 400 MHz USRP X410 OFDM configuration: * - MCR = 491.52 MS/s * - CE = 266.667 MHz * - NIPC = 2 * - N_FFT=8192, N_CP=576 (subcarrier spacing = 60 kHz)
* *
* \f$ T_{Lat}≈N_{Lat}/CE≈17536/(266.667⋅10e6) s≈66us \f$ *
* * ## Time Stamping * * CHDR data packets containing FFT input or output data can comprise time * stamps. The figure below illustrates how these are computed for the example: * - OFDM configuration – cyclic prefixes are added or removed * - Burst containing 2 OFDM symbols * * * * 1. For the first packet in a burst:
* The incoming packet contains a time stamp for the start of the burst * that is provided by either the host or the radio block. This time stamp * is in units of the master clock rate. The packet at the output of the * (i)FFT block contains this exact same timestamp without modifications. * In other words, the FFT block doesn't adjust the timestamp to * compensate for the insertion or removal of the cyclic prefix. * 2. For subsequent packets in a burst:
* The FFT block increments the time stamp for the start of the burst by * the number of samples per packet (SPP). * * * ## Behavior In Case of Streaming Errors * * It is possible that transmit or receive packets are dropped on the transport * link that connects USRP and host computer. This section discusses how to * detect and handle * these situations. * * RX Streaming (FFT operation) * 1. After each receive call, check for the out_of_sequence flag in the stream * metadata. * 2. If the receive call metadata indicates the data is out of sequence, then * the application can use the time_spec to determine where this data goes in * the sequence of expected data and take appropriate action (e.g., discard * the data up to the start of the next symbol). * 3. Streaming can continue as before after this point. * * TX Streaming (iFFT operation) * 1. The application sets up a thread to receive and monitor asynchronous * messages ( rev_async_msg() ) from the TX streamer. * 2. When an async message reports an underflow or sequence error, then the * application should end the current burst and wait for the burst ack. * - The FFT block will automatically end the data stream and flush any data * out of the FFT block. This may cause the last packet to be sent with * corrupted data, since part of an input symbol was lost, but should * leave the FFT block in a good state for the next burst. * - If the application doesn't receive a burst ack, then it should reset * the FFT block, and reconfigure it before proceeding. * 3. After this the application can restart streaming from where it left off * or at some later point in time as needed by the application. * * * * ## Timed Commands * The FFT block does not support timed commands. That is, none of its * runtime-configurable properties can be configured in a deterministically timed * fashion. * * ## Configuring the CHDR Packet Lengths for Initial Debugging * Debugging can be simplified by configuring the CHDR packet length such that * an integer multiple of the CHDR packet length equals the FFT size. * The FFT functionality does not break if this recommendation is not followed. * Example: In case of a 4096 point FFT, the CHDR packet length could be chosen * equal to 1024 samples per packet. * * * # Known Limitations * 1. MAX_FFT_SIZE_LOG2 = 16 (64k FFT) can be configured but has not been * validated in hardware * 2. MAX_FFT_SIZE_LOG2 = 15 (32k FFT): using this maximum-size FFT * implementation in an 8 point FFT configuration does not work properly. * */ // clang-format on class UHD_API fft_block_control : public uhd::rfnoc::noc_block_base { public: RFNOC_DECLARE_BLOCK(fft_block_control) //! Register addresses static const uint32_t REG_COMPAT_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_PORT_CONFIG_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CAPABILITIES_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CAPABILITIES2_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_RESET_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_LENGTH_LOG2_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_SCALING_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_DIRECTION_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CP_INS_LEN_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CP_INS_LIST_LOAD_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CP_INS_LIST_CLR_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CP_INS_LIST_OCC_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CP_REM_LEN_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CP_REM_LIST_LOAD_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CP_REM_LIST_CLR_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_CP_REM_LIST_OCC_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_OVERFLOW_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_BYPASS_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_ORDER_ADDR; static const uint32_t REG_MAGNITUDE_ADDR; //! Register addresses of the FFT block version 1 static const uint32_t REG_RESET_ADDR_V1; static const uint32_t REG_LENGTH_LOG2_ADDR_V1; static const uint32_t REG_MAGNITUDE_ADDR_V1; static const uint32_t REG_DIRECTION_ADDR_V1; static const uint32_t REG_SCALING_ADDR_V1; static const uint32_t REG_ORDER_ADDR_V1; /*! Set the FFT direction * * Sets the direction of the FFT, either forward (FORWARD) or inverse * (REVERSE). * * \param direction FFT direction */ virtual void set_direction(const fft_direction direction) = 0; /*! Get the FFT direction * * Returns the current direction of the FFT. * * \returns FFT direction */ virtual fft_direction get_direction() const = 0; /*! Set the format of the returned FFT output data * * Sets the format in which the FFT output data is returned. The following * formats are supported: * * * amplitude/phase data (COMPLEX) * * magnitude data (MAGNITUDE) * * mag-squared data (MAGNITUDE_SQUARED) * * \param magnitude Format of the returned FFT output data */ virtual void set_magnitude(const fft_magnitude magnitude) = 0; /*! Get the format of the returned FFT output data * * Returns the current output format of the FFT data. * * \returns Format of the returned FFT output data */ virtual fft_magnitude get_magnitude() const = 0; /*! Set the shift configuration of the output FFT data * * Sets how the FFT output data is shifted (to get the zero frequency bin * to the center of the output data). The following output data shift * configurations are supported: * * * Negative frequencies first, then positive frequencies (NORMAL) * * Positive frequencies first, then negative frequencies (REVERSE) * * Bypass the shift altogether, leaving the zero frequency bin * returned first (NATURAL). * * Bit-reversed order (BIT_REVERSE). This is typically the native * order output by the FFT. In other words, selecting this mode may * mean that the data from the FFT is not reordered. In this mode, * the indices of the FFT are bit-reversed. For example, for a size * 16 FFT, instead of outputting bins in the order 0000, 0001, 0010, * 0011, etc., it outputs bins 0000, 1000, 0100, 1100, etc. * * \param shift Configuration for shifting FFT output data */ virtual void set_shift_config(const fft_shift shift) = 0; /*! Get the shift configuration of the output FFT data * * Returns the current shift configuration of the output FFT data. * * \returns Shift configuration of the output FFT data */ virtual fft_shift get_shift_config() const = 0; /*! Set the scaling factor for the FFT block * * This is a convenience function which can be used instead of * set_scaling(). Based on the given factor, it automatically sets the * scaling mask by evenly distributing the scaling across the active FFT * stages * * Examples: * - factor = 1.0 -> no scaling * - factor = 0.5 (1/2) -> the scaling will be set to 0b000000000001 * -> scale by 2 in the first FFT stage * - factor = 0.0625 (1/16) -> the scaling will be set to 0b000000001010 * -> scale by 4 in both the first and the second FFT stage * - factor = 0.03125 (1/32) -> the scaling will be set to 0b000000011010 * -> scale by 4 in both the first and the second FFT stage * and by 2 in the third FFT stage * * \param factor Desired scaling factor */ virtual void set_scaling_factor(const double factor) = 0; /*! Set the scaling schedule for the FFT block * * Sets the scaling for each stage of the FFT. This value maps directly * to the scale schedule field in the configuration channel data that is * passed to the Xilinx AXI FFT IP. For more information on the format * of this data, see Xilinx document PG109, Fast Fourier Transform * LogiCORE IP Product Guide. * * Examples: * - scaling = 0b000000000000 -> no scaling * - scaling = 0b000000000001 -> scale by 2 in the first FFT stage * - scaling = 0b000000001010 -> scale by 4 in both the first and the second * FFT stage * - scaling = 0b000000011010 -> scale by 4 in both the first and the second * FFT stage and by 2 in the third FFT stage * * \param scaling Scaling schedule for the FFT block */ virtual void set_scaling(const uint32_t scaling) = 0; /*! Get the scaling schedule for the FFT block * * Returns the current scaling schedule for the FFT block. * * \returns Scaling schedule for the FFT block */ virtual uint32_t get_scaling() const = 0; /*! Set the length of the FFT * * Sets the length of the FFT in number of samples. Note that the FFT * IP requires a power-of-two number of samples; the incoming value will * be coerced to the closest smaller power of two. * * \param length Desired FFT length */ virtual void set_length(const uint32_t length) = 0; /*! Get the length of the FFT * * Returns the current length of the FFT. * * \returns Current FFT length */ virtual uint32_t get_length() const = 0; /*! Set the bypass mode of the FFT * * Enable FFT bypass mode. Set true to enable, false to disable. When * enabled, the data is passed through without any FFT processing. Note that * cyclic prefix insertion will not work in bypass mode, because insertion * is handled by the FFT core, but cyclic prefix removal will work. * * \param bypass FFT bypass moe */ virtual void set_bypass_mode(const bool bypass) = 0; /*! Get the bypass mode of the FFT * * Returns the current bypass mode. * * \returns Current FFT bypass mode */ virtual bool get_bypass_mode() const = 0; /*! Get the number of items per clock cycle (NIPC) * * Returns the number of items per clock cycle (NIPC) that this block is * configured to process. Packet sizes and cyclic prefix lengths must a * multiple of this value. * * \returns NIPC */ virtual uint32_t get_nipc() const = 0; /*! Get the maximum supported length of the FFT * * Returns the maximum supported length of the FFT. * * \returns Maximum supported FFT length */ virtual uint32_t get_max_length() const = 0; /*! Get the maximum supported cyclic prefix length * * Returns the maximum supported cyclic prefix length. * * \returns Maximum supported cyclic prefix length */ virtual uint32_t get_max_cp_length() const = 0; /*! Get the maximum supported number of values that can be written to the * cyclic prefix removal list * * Returns the maximum supported number of values that can be written to * the cyclic prefix removal list. * * \returns Maximum number of values for the cyclic prefix removal list */ virtual uint32_t get_max_cp_removal_list_length() const = 0; /*! Get the maximum supported number of values that can be written to the * cyclic prefix insertion list * * Returns the maximum supported number of values that can be written to * the cyclic prefix insertion list. * * \returns Maximum number of values for the cyclic prefix insertion list */ virtual uint32_t get_max_cp_insertion_list_length() const = 0; /*! Load values to the cyclic prefix insertion list. * * Loads values to the cyclic prefix insertion list. Each value represents * the length of a cyclic prefix that is prepended to the output of the * (typically inverse) FFT operation (typically the time domain signal). If * the length of the cyclic prefix insertion list is m, then the cyclic * prefix length that is added to the output signal of symbol n is: * * cp_length[n] = cp_length[n mod m] * * \param cp_lengths The cyclic prefix lengths to be written to the list */ virtual void set_cp_insertion_list(const std::vector cp_lengths) = 0; /*! Gets the values from the cyclic prefix insertion list. * * Gets values to the cyclic prefix insertion list. After initialization, * no CP insertion values are configured. Use the function * set_cp_insertion_list to set the values. */ virtual std::vector get_cp_insertion_list() const = 0; /*! Load values to the cyclic prefix removal list. * * Loads values to the cyclic prefix removal list. Each value represents * the length of a cyclic prefix that is removed from the input signal * (typically the time domain signal) before performing the (typically * forward) FFT operation. If the length of the cyclic prefix removal list * is m, then the cyclic prefix length that is removed from the input signal * of symbol n is: * * cp_length[n] = cp_length[n mod m] * * \param cp_lengths The cyclic prefix lengths to be written to the list */ virtual void set_cp_removal_list(const std::vector cp_lengths) = 0; /*! Gets the values from the cyclic prefix removal list. * * Gets values to the cyclic prefix removal list. After initialization, * no CP removal values are configured. Use the function * set_cp_removal_list to set the values. */ virtual std::vector get_cp_removal_list() const = 0; }; }} // namespace uhd::rfnoc
